key_insight: A gradient-enhanced damage model uses an endurance-surface concept to capture fatigue damage accumulation over many cycles, including anisotropic and multiaxial effects.
engineering_relevance: The framework offers a thermodynamically consistent way to predict high-cycle fatigue and quasi-brittle failure in concrete and metals with mesh-objective simulations.
method: Uses the energy-release rate as the driving force for damage evolution.
Defines damage growth by the distance of the driving force from an endurance surface, enabling an explicit endurance limit.
Adds micromorphic gradient regularization plus anisotropic damage and microcrack closure/reopening effects.
evidence_signal: The model is calibrated against monotonic concrete response and high-cycle fatigue data for low-alloy steel, then tested on L-shaped concrete failure, stress-life curves, and axial-torsional fatigue.
watch_item: The added anisotropy, closure-reopening behavior, and micromorphic regularization may increase calibration and implementation complexity for new materials.
paper_title: Modeling Fatigue-Induced Anisotropic Quasi-Brittle Damage Based on the Endurance Surface Concept
